food_subgroup: Vegetable curry — nut and coconut-based gravy

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Veg Korma; mapped to broader 'curry dish' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Korma node; A0CKP (cooked mixed vegetable dish) is the closest defensible match. Nut-and-coconut gravy component is not captured in this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Veg Korma or analogous Indian vegetable curry with nut-based gravy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant or home serving of Veg Korma is approximately 250 g, typically served alongside naan, roti, or steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN) / ICMR, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Veg Korma are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods using component-level data for mixed cooked vegetables, cashew nuts, coconut, and spices. No single exact Veg Korma entry exists in the NIN table; values represent a weighted composite estimate consistent with the described recipe. Saturated fat estimated from cashew and coconut fat fraction data in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 12087, 12104) as NIN does not report fatty acid fractions for this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate for mixed vegetable curry with legumes and nuts
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Veg Korma. GI of 40 is estimated by analogy to mixed vegetable curries with legumes and nut-based gravies reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care). The high dietary fibre (3.4 g/100 g) from mixed vegetables and the fat content from cashews and coconut slow gastric emptying and glucose absorption, supporting a low-GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 28.0) / 100 = 11.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 280 mg/100 g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 700 mg sodium, which is 30% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and avoid adding extra salt or ghee; the potassium content (245 mg/100 g) from vegetables partially offsets sodium load, but portion control remains important.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI (~40) driven by high dietary fibre (3.4 g/100 g) and fat from cashews and coconut slowing glucose absorption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Veg Korma is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when served in a standard 250 g portion alongside whole-grain bread or brown rice; pairing with refined naan raises the overall meal GI and should be moderated.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.2 g/100 g) from coconut and cashews; however, the dish is cholesterol-free and provides heart-healthy monounsaturated fats from cashews.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume Veg Korma in moderate portions (≤250 g), preferring preparations that use minimal ghee and limit coconut cream to control saturated fat intake below 7% of daily energy.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cardamom and ginger have mild anti-inflammatory properties; antioxidant-rich vegetables support immune modulation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Veg Korma is generally well-tolerated in rheumatoid arthritis; individuals sensitive to nightshades should request preparations without potatoes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Veg Korma is a mild, creamy Indian vegetable curry with a white-to-pale-yellow gravy characteristic of Mughlai and South Indian hotel-style cooking. The dish is built on a nut-and-coconut paste base made from cashews, poppy seeds, and grated coconut, which is slow-simmered with a medley of seasonal vegetables — typically cauliflower, carrots, green beans, and green peas — until the gravy achieves a silky, emulsified consistency. Aromatic spices including green cardamom, cloves, and mace provide fragrance without significant chili heat, making Korma one of the mildest Indian curries. The flavour profile is nutty, subtly sweet from the vegetables, and richly aromatic. Nutritionally, a 100 g serving provides approximately 135 kcal, 9.5 g fat (predominantly from cashews and coconut), 11.2 g carbohydrate, 2.8 g protein, and 3.4 g dietary fibre. The estimated glycemic index is low (~40), making it suitable for blood sugar management when paired with whole-grain accompaniments. Sodium is moderate at 280 mg/100 g and should be monitored in hypertension. The dish is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-compatible. It is a popular choice for festive vegetarian menus, family dinners, and as a mild alternative to spicier curries, classically served with naan, missi roti, or aromatic pulao.
common_user_queries: Is Veg Korma good for diabetics?
How many calories in Veg Korma?
What is the GI of Vegetable Korma?
Is Veg Korma suitable for hypertension?
What vegetables are in Veg Korma?
Difference between Veg Korma and Navratan Korma

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component-level data for Indian foods; no single exact Veg Korma entry exists in any approved primary source. Saturated fat and fatty acid fractions are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cashew and coconut data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar mixed vegetable curries. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.2 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.8 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cashew (NDB 12087) and coconut (NDB 12104) fatty acid profiles proportional to recipe composition, as ICMR-NIN does not report fatty acid fractions for this dish. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite Veg Korma preparation is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing B12; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN plant food data. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no animal fat or egg ingredients are present in the standard recipe. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0: no hydrogenated fats are used in the traditional preparation. 6. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Veg Korma was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 7. The input record contained four duplicate portion entries (all 250 g); deduplicated to a single default serving of 250 g. 8. The input source 'Cashew nuts and health outcomes, Nutrients Journal' is not on the approved source list and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for fatty acid estimation. 9. Vitamin B12 at 0.2 mcg in the original input is biologically implausible for a fully plant-based dish with no dairy or egg; corrected to 0.0 with justification above.
